section.title: The Prayer of Faith
section.sectionRationale: The letter closes as it has run throughout, calling faith into action, now in the practice of prayer and the recovery of the straying. James commands prayer in every circumstance, directs the sick to the elders, promises much from the prayer of faith, holds up Elijah as proof that ordinary people's prayers move heaven, and ends with the great work of turning a wanderer back to the truth. There is no formal farewell; the last word is a rescued soul.
section.thesis: Prayer is the fitting response to every season of life, and the believing, righteous prayer of God's people, offered together and even for the straying, has real power to heal, to forgive, and to save a soul from death.
section.frameNote: Three movements: prayer for every circumstance and for the sick; the powerful prayer of the righteous, proven by Elijah; and the saving work of restoring a wanderer.

units.label: Prayer for every season
units.note: the sick, the elders, and the oil

verses.text: Is any one of you suffering? He should {{proseuchomai|pray}}. Is anyone cheerful? He should sing praises.
Is any one of you sick? He should call the {{presbyteroi|elders}} of the church to pray over him and anoint him with oil in the name of the Lord.
And the {{euche|prayer offered in faith}} will restore the one who is sick. The Lord will raise him up. If he has sinned, he will be forgiven.

units.label: The powerful prayer of the righteous
units.note: Elijah, a man just like us

verses.text: Therefore confess your sins to each other and pray for each other so that you may be healed. The prayer of a {{dikaios|righteous man}} has great power to prevail.
Elijah was a man just like us. He prayed earnestly that it would not rain, and it did not rain on the land for three and a half years.
Again he prayed, and the heavens gave rain, and the earth yielded its crops.
My brothers, if one of you should wander from the truth and someone should bring him back,
consider this: Whoever turns a sinner from the error of his way will save his soul from death and cover over a multitude of sins.

proseuchomai.gloss: To pray, to address God in petition and worship. James makes prayer the natural response to every condition: the suffering pray, the cheerful sing praise, the sick call for prayer. Prayer runs through the whole passage as the lifeline of faith in every season.

presbyteroi.gloss: Elders, the recognized spiritual leaders of a local church. The sick are to call them to pray and anoint with oil in the Lord's name, showing that healing is sought through the ministry of the church, not a lone healer. The elders act as shepherds interceding for the flock.

euche.gloss: A prayer or vow; here the prayer offered in faith that restores the sick. James stresses that it is faith, not the ritual, that God honors, and that the Lord Himself raises the person up. The confidence rests on God's power and will, not on a technique.

dikaios.gloss: Righteous, upright, right with God. The prayer of a righteous person has great power in its working. Elijah is offered as proof: a man of like nature to us whose earnest, righteous prayer shut and opened the heavens. Righteousness here is not sinless perfection but a life rightly related to God through faith.

basic.context: James ends by weaving everything back to prayer. Whatever your situation, pray: if you are suffering, pray; if you are glad, sing; if you are sick, call the church's elders to pray over you and anoint you with oil in the Lord's name. He promises that the prayer of faith will restore the sick, that the Lord will raise them up, and that sins will be forgiven. So confess your sins to each other and pray for each other. To prove that ordinary prayers matter, he points to Elijah, a man just like us, whose prayers stopped and started the rain. And he closes with a call to go after those who wander from the truth, because bringing a straying sinner back saves a soul from death.
basic.hermeneutics: This is intensely practical, prescriptive teaching about the life of prayer in the church. The main interpretive care concerns verses 14 to 15 on healing: James promises much from the prayer of faith, yet the whole Bible, and James himself with his stress on patient suffering, keeps this within God's sovereign will. The anointing with oil is done in the name of the Lord as an act of consecrated prayer, not magic. The closing verses show that James cares supremely about the eternal rescue of the soul, not only physical restoration.
basic.originalLanguages: The passage is stitched together by proseuchomai, to pray, the response to every season. The sick call the presbyteroi, the elders. It is the euche, the prayer of faith, that restores, God honoring faith rather than ritual. And the prayer of the dikaios, the righteous person, has great power, proven by Elijah.
basic.typology: The anointing with oil in the Lord's name connects to the Spirit and to Christ, the Anointed One, in whose name the church prays. Elijah, whose prayer shut and opened the heavens, foreshadows the greater intercession of Christ and the power of prayer in His name. Saving a soul from death and covering a multitude of sins points to the atoning, restoring work of Christ, in whom sins are truly covered.
basic.christ: Everything here is done in the name of the Lord, that is, in the name of Jesus, through whom the church prays and is heard. Christ is the great intercessor whose prayers never fail, and He is the Savior whose blood covers the multitude of sins that turning a wanderer back brings under His mercy. The One who raises up the sick is the risen Lord who will finally raise all His people.
basic.god: God is the hearer and answerer of prayer in every circumstance, the Lord who raises up the sick and forgives sin. He honors the faith-filled prayer of ordinary righteous people, as He did Elijah's. He is compassionate toward the straying and delights when a sinner is turned back, for He is the God who saves souls from death. Prayer works because He is powerful, present, and merciful.
basic.crossReferences: Elijah's prayers about the rain are recorded in 1 Kings 17 to 18. Jesus taught persistent, believing prayer, Matthew 7:7-11 and Mark 11:24. Covering a multitude of sins echoes Proverbs 10:12 and 1 Peter 4:8. And Galatians 6:1 speaks of gently restoring one caught in sin.
basic.oneStory: From Elijah on Carmel to the praying church, God has always acted through the prayers of His people and pursued the wanderer to bring him home. James ends the letter not with a farewell but with a rescued soul, drawing together its great theme: faith that lives, prays, and loves. All of it is in the name of the Lord Jesus, the intercessor and Savior who raises the sick, forgives sin, and covers a multitude of transgressions, and who is bringing many wandering souls back to the truth.

context.literary: The letter ends without the customary greetings or benediction, closing instead on the note of restoring a wanderer, which fits its practical, sermonic character. The section is unified by prayer: for the suffering, the joyful, the sick, and one another, capped by the example of Elijah and the pastoral appeal to reclaim the straying, which sums up the aim of the whole letter.
context.historical: James writes to congregations led by elders, in a setting where anointing the sick with oil was a familiar act with both medicinal and symbolic associations, here given a spiritual frame as prayer in the Lord's name. The concern for members wandering from the truth reflects real pressure on scattered believers to drift under trial, worldliness, or false teaching.
context.canonical: The passage draws the Old Testament example of Elijah into Christian practice and echoes Jesus' teaching on believing prayer. Its language of covering a multitude of sins links to Proverbs and Peter, and its vision of the community praying, confessing, and restoring one another expresses the New Testament ideal of the church as a body that bears one another's burdens.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To make prayer the reflex of faith in every circumstance, to direct the sick to the praying ministry of the elders and to mutual confession and intercession, to assure believers that the earnest prayer of the righteous has real power, and to commend the saving work of turning a wanderer back to the truth.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage is largely prescriptive, a series of commands and directions about prayer, confession, and restoration, supported by the descriptive example of Elijah and the promise of what God does in response. Its abiding call is to a praying, confessing, mutually restoring church that seeks the healing of bodies and the rescue of souls.

debates.question: Does verse 15 guarantee that the prayer of faith always heals?

views.label: It is a real promise held within God's sovereign will
views.case: James genuinely promises much from the prayer of faith, and the church should pray boldly for the sick expecting God to act. Yet Scripture as a whole, and James's own call to patient suffering, keep such prayer submitted to God's wisdom; not every faithful prayer results in physical healing, as even Paul's thorn shows. The certainty rests on God's power and goodness, not on a guaranteed technique.

views.label: The restoring and raising up point ultimately to salvation
views.case: Others note that the words for restore and raise up can carry the sense of spiritual restoration and final resurrection, and that forgiveness is expressly included. On this reading the promise embraces the whole person, with bodily healing as God wills and the deeper healing of forgiveness and resurrection assured.

debates.bearing: Both guard against two errors: treating the verse as a magic formula that always heals, and emptying it of real confidence in prayer. The church prays in faith, expects God to work, and rests the outcome in His hands.

debates.question: What is the point of the Elijah example in verses 17 to 18?

views.label: Ordinary people's prayers have extraordinary power
views.case: James stresses that Elijah was a man just like us, of the same weak nature, so his answered prayers are not beyond reach. The lesson is that the earnest prayer of any righteous believer, not a spiritual elite, can move the hand of God.

views.label: Righteousness and earnestness matter in prayer
views.case: Others highlight that Elijah prayed earnestly and was righteous, connecting to verse 16. The example encourages fervent, believing prayer from those walking rightly with God, without implying prayer is a mechanical lever.

debates.bearing: Either way the example democratizes prayer: it invites every ordinary believer to pray earnestly and expectantly, confident that God hears and acts.

study.originalLanguages: The whole passage is held together by proseuchomai, to pray, the reflex of faith for the suffering, the sick, and one another. The sick summon the presbyteroi, the elders, who pray and anoint in the name of the Lord, locating healing in the ministry of the church rather than a solitary healer. What restores is the euche, the prayer of faith, so that the emphasis falls on faith and on the Lord who raises up, not on the oil as ritual. And the ground of confidence is that the prayer of a dikaios, a righteous person rightly related to God, has great power in its working, proven by Elijah, a man of like nature to us, whose earnest prayers shut and reopened the heavens.

typology.type: Anointing with oil in the name of the Lord
typology.fulfillment: Points to the Spirit and to Christ the Anointed One, in whose name the praying church acts.

typology.type: Elijah's prayer shutting and opening the heavens
typology.fulfillment: Foreshadows the mighty, prevailing intercession offered in Christ's name, and Christ's own perfect intercession, Hebrews 7:25.

typology.type: Saving a soul and covering a multitude of sins
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ's atoning work, in which sins are truly covered and wanderers are brought home, 1 Peter 4:8.

study.christ: Every act here is done in the name of the Lord Jesus, in whose name the church prays, anoints, and is heard. He is the great and righteous intercessor whose prayer never fails and who lives to intercede for His people, so that our praying is caught up into His. He is the risen Lord who raises up the sick and will raise all His own at the last day, and He is the Savior whose blood covers the multitude of sins that a restored wanderer brings under mercy. The letter's final vision, a soul saved from death, is the very work of Christ.
study.god: God is the ever-present hearer of prayer, the Lord who raises up the sick, forgives the sinner, and answers the earnest cry of ordinary righteous people, as He answered Elijah. He is compassionate toward those who stray and rejoices when a wanderer is turned back, for He is the God who saves souls from death. Prayer has power not in itself but because He is mighty, near, and merciful, delighting to act for and through His praying people.

crossReferences.note: Elijah's prayers that shut and then reopened the heavens, the example James cites in verses 17 to 18.
Whatever you ask in prayer, believe that you have received it, on the believing prayer James commends.
Love covers a multitude of sins, echoing the language of verse 20.
Restore one caught in sin gently, matching the work of turning a wanderer back.
Christ always lives to intercede, the perfect ground of the church's confidence in prayer.

study.oneStory: From Elijah calling down fire and rain to the praying, confessing church, God has always worked through the prayers of His people and gone after the wanderer to bring him home. James ends his letter not with a polite farewell but with a rescued soul, gathering up its whole message: a faith that truly lives will pray, confess, and love enough to reclaim the straying. All of this is in the name of the Lord Jesus, the righteous intercessor and Savior who raises the sick, forgives sin, and covers a multitude of transgressions, and who is even now bringing many wandering souls back to the truth.

quiz.q: What is James's counsel for the different seasons of life in verse 13?

options.text: The suffering should pray, and the cheerful should sing praises.
options.note: Verse 13: prayer and praise as the response to every circumstance.

options.text: Every situation should turn us toward God in prayer or praise.
options.note: True to the thrust, if less specific than the verse.

options.text: We should only pray when things are going badly.
options.note: James includes praise in good times, not prayer only in trouble.

options.text: We should handle each season on our own strength.
options.note: The whole point is to bring every season to God.

quiz.q: What does James direct the sick person to do?

options.text: Call the elders to pray over him and anoint him with oil in the Lord's name.
options.note: Verse 14: healing sought through the praying ministry of the church.

options.text: Seek the prayer of the church's leaders in the name of the Lord.
options.note: True to the verse, capturing the corporate, prayerful nature.

options.text: Find a single gifted healer to perform a ritual.
options.note: James points to the elders together, not a lone healer or ritual.

options.text: Keep the illness private and tell no one.
options.note: The opposite: call the elders and confess to one another.

quiz.q: How should we understand the promise that the prayer of faith restores the sick?

options.text: A real promise to pray boldly, held within God's sovereign will and including forgiveness.
options.note: The confidence rests on God's power and goodness, not a guaranteed technique.

options.text: The restoring and raising up embrace the whole person, as God wills.
options.note: True, noting the words can reach to spiritual restoration and resurrection.

options.text: A guarantee that every faithful prayer always brings physical healing.
options.note: Scripture and James's call to patient suffering qualify this; it is not a formula.

options.text: A statement that prayer for the sick is pointless.
options.note: James urges such prayer with real expectation that God acts.

quiz.q: Why does James point to Elijah in verses 17 to 18?

options.text: To show that an ordinary person's earnest, righteous prayer has great power.
options.note: Verse 17: Elijah was a man just like us, yet his prayers moved heaven.

options.text: To encourage fervent, believing prayer from those walking rightly with God.
options.note: True, linking to the righteous man's prayer in verse 16.

options.text: To prove that only unique prophets can expect answered prayer.
options.note: The stress is that Elijah was just like us, so prayer is for all.

options.text: To show that prayer is a mechanical lever that forces God's hand.
options.note: Prayer prevails because God hears, not by mechanical technique.

quiz.q: What does James say about turning a wanderer back from error?

options.text: It saves the sinner's soul from death and covers a multitude of sins.
options.note: Verse 20: restoring the straying is a soul-saving work.

options.text: Bringing back one who strays from the truth is of eternal importance.
options.note: True to the weight James gives it as the letter's closing note.

options.text: Wanderers are best left alone to sort themselves out.
options.note: James calls the church to go after and reclaim them.

options.text: Only ordained ministers may seek to restore a straying believer.
options.note: James says whoever turns a sinner back, addressing all the brothers.

questions: James makes prayer the response to every season. Do you instinctively turn to God in both suffering and joy, or only in one of them?
The sick are told to call the elders and the church to confess and pray together. What keeps you from inviting others into your struggles, and what would openness look like?
Elijah was a man just like us, yet his prayers were powerful. How does that change the way you view your own praying?
The letter ends by urging us to bring back those who wander from the truth. Is there someone drifting from Christ whom God may be calling you to pursue in love?
Looking back over the whole letter of James, which single call, on trials, speech, wealth, wisdom, or prayer, is God most pressing on you to obey?

translationNotes.note: Anoint him with oil renders a practice done in the name of the Lord; it frames the act as consecrated, prayerful ministry through the elders, not a magical rite.

Restore and raise him up can carry both physical and spiritual senses; the prayer offered in faith renders euche, stressing faith and the Lord's action rather than ritual, with forgiveness expressly included.

Has great power to prevail renders a phrase about the prayer of a righteous person being powerful in its working; the term dikaios, righteous, denotes a life rightly related to God, not sinless perfection.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for James 5:13-20. No em or en dashes occur in this movement.
